questions.question: What is the core difference between rule-based and reason-based alignment?
options: Rule-based is for chatbots, reason-based for agents

Rule-based lists every disallowed thing (fast, auditable, impossible to keep current, over-refuses); reason-based encodes principles the model reasons from (scales to unseen cases, harder to audit, fails by misapplying a principle)
Reason-based requires no training
Rule-based uses a bigger model
questions.explanation: No rule list is both long enough for the long tail and short enough for fast inference. The 2026 Claude Constitution takes a middle position: hardcoded prohibitions are rule-based; everything else is reason-based.

questions.question: What is the four-tier priority hierarchy in the 2026 Constitution, highest first?
options: Helpfulness, ethics, safety, guidelines
Safety and supporting human oversight; ethics; Anthropic guidelines; helpfulness
Speed, accuracy, cost, style
User, operator, Anthropic, model
questions.explanation: Higher tiers win conflicts — the same shape as Unix priorities or network QoS. Tier 1 is specifically "do not act in ways that make human oversight harder," not generic caution; helpfulness is deliberately last.

questions.question: What is the difference between hardcoded prohibitions and soft-coded defaults?
options: Hardcoded ones are written in C

Hardcoded prohibitions (CBRN uplift, CSAM, infrastructure attacks) cannot be overridden by operator or user; soft-coded defaults (length, scope, style, tool patterns) are operator-adjustable within declared bounds
Soft-coded rules apply only on weekends
Hardcoded rules expire after each session
questions.explanation: Wrongness that doesn't depend on context is hardcoded and enforced at the weights level where possible, the inference layer where not. Operators adjust defaults inside a bound but cannot rename away a prohibition.

questions.question: How did the original 2022 Constitutional AI train harmlessness?
options: Pure human labeling of every response
Generate responses, have the model critique each against the constitution, revise based on the critique, then RLAIF on the revised pairs
Filtering the pretraining corpus
A separate classifier blocking outputs
questions.explanation: Self-critique plus RLAIF (RL from AI feedback) produced a model that refuses with principled explanations rather than blanket refusals. The 2026 Constitution uses a descendant plus post-training on the explicit tier hierarchy.

questions.question: What is the honest caveat about reason-based alignment the lesson stresses?
options: It is slower than rule-based alignment

It relies on the model generalizing principles to unanticipated situations — and can drift in interpretation, miss principle-conflict cases, or be exploited via "the user asked, so helpfulness says yes"
It cannot handle multiple languages
It only works on Anthropic models
questions.explanation: Interpretation drift over training cycles connects to the alignment-anchor failure mode from Lesson 8. The lesson also notes the 2023 participatory experiment found ~50% divergence between public and corporate principles — findings the 2026 version did not incorporate.
